pi_items.source: Downloaded from https://wiki.wireshark.org/uploads/27707187aeb30df68e70c8fb9d614981/http.cap and verified with file command
Task description specifies saving to /home/ga/Documents/captures/protocol_hierarchy.txt; export_result.sh checks for this filename
tshark -r http.cap -Y eth | wc -l = 43; protocol hierarchy shows eth as top-level protocol
tshark -r http.cap -Y ip | wc -l = 43; protocol hierarchy shows ip under eth
tshark -r http.cap -Y tcp | wc -l = 41; protocol hierarchy shows tcp under ip
tshark -r http.cap -Y http | wc -l = 4; protocol hierarchy shows http under tcp
tshark -r http.cap -Y udp | wc -l = 2; protocol hierarchy shows udp under ip
tshark -r http.cap -Y dns | wc -l = 2; protocol hierarchy shows dns under udp
Downloaded file size verification: 26K per ls -lh

privileged_info_summary: The http.cap file is a real, publicly available sample PCAP file from Wireshark's official wiki (https://wiki.wireshark.org/SampleCaptures). The file contains 43 frames demonstrating HTTP protocol communication with the following protocol stack: Ethernet (43 frames), IP (43 frames), TCP (41 frames), HTTP (4 frames), UDP (2 frames), and DNS (2 frames). The file is 26 KB in size. The verifier checks for the creation of protocol_hierarchy.txt output file and validates that it contains references to Ethernet, IP, TCP, HTTP protocols and percentage/count data.
